did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Vision Transformer (ViT Base) as the baseline model and implemented a multi-stage mitigation pipeline including hybrid skin tone clustering, targeted augmentation, re-sampling, fairness-aware loss, and adversarial debiasing to evaluate diagnostic performance and fairness in melanoma detection using ISIC 2020 and HAM10000 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and implementation of a multi-stage mitigation pipeline (hybrid skin tone clustering, targeted augmentation, re-sampling, fairness-aware loss, and adversarial debiasing) using a Vision Transformer baseline on ISIC 2020 and HAM10000 datasets. It reports new quantitative results, including specific improvements in sensitivity, AUC, and fairness metrics, and includes a methodology section detailing the proposed framework and experimental configurations.

title: Mitigating Skin Tone Bias in AI Driven Melanoma Detection to Improve Diagnostic Equity Across Diverse Populations
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on AI-Driven Smart Systems and Ubiquitous Computing (ICAUC-2026)
brief_summary: This study addresses skin tone bias in AI-driven melanoma detection by implementing a multi-stage mitigation pipeline using ISIC 2020 and HAM10000 datasets. The authors employed a Vision Transformer (ViT Base) baseline enhanced with hybrid skin tone clustering, targeted augmentation, re-sampling, fairness-aware loss, and adversarial debiasing. Evaluation across Light, Medium, and Dark skin groups demonstrated that the fairness-enhanced model increased dark skin sensitivity by 7.1% and boosted overall AUC to 0.931. Additionally, the approach reduced major fairness disparities, including accuracy gap and equalized odds difference, by approximately 50%. Statistical tests confirmed significant reductions in diagnostic inequality, indicating that the proposed method optimizes both clinical performance and equity for diverse populations.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary diagnostic target of the study, evaluated for detection performance and fairness across skin tones using ISIC 2020 and HAM10000 datasets.
Used as the negative class for binary classification tasks in the ISIC 2020 and HAM10000 datasets, which comprise annotated dermoscopic images for melanoma and non-melanoma lesions.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopic images into melanoma and non-melanoma (benign) categories using a Vision Transformer (ViT Base). The model outputs melanoma probability (y_hat) for diagnostic prediction. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, and AUC across Light, Medium, and Dark skin tone groups.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset comprising annotated dermoscopic images for melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The paper notes it has underrepresentation of darker skin tones.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training the Vision Transformer model after applying targeted augmentation and re-sampling to address skin tone imbalance.
use.testing: Used for evaluating diagnostic performance and fairness metrics (sensitivity, AUC, accuracy gap, etc.) across Light, Medium, and Dark skin tone groups.
use.other: Combined with HAM10000 for the study.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset comprising annotated dermoscopic images for melanoma and non-melanoma lesions. The paper notes it has underrepresentation of darker skin tones.

use.training: Used as part of the combined dataset for training the Vision Transformer model after applying targeted augmentation and re-sampling to address skin tone imbalance.
use.testing: Used for evaluating diagnostic performance and fairness metrics (sensitivity, AUC, accuracy gap, etc.) across Light, Medium, and Dark skin tone groups.
use.other: Combined with ISIC 2020 for the study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To confirm significant reductions in diagnostic inequality and validate bias removal.
To confirm significant reductions in diagnostic inequality and validate bias removal.
To ensure reliable unbiased evaluation and preserve balanced skin tone representation across all folds.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in diagnostic performance or fairness metrics between the baseline model and the proposed fairness-enhanced model (i.e., the mitigation pipeline did not significantly reduce diagnostic inequality).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant reduction in diagnostic inequality and improvement in fairness metrics (such as sensitivity and accuracy gap) for the proposed fairness-enhanced model compared to the baseline, particularly for darker skin tones.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Statistical tests (McNemar's test and ANOVA) confirmed significant reductions in diagnostic inequality, validating that the proposed method effectively mitigates skin tone bias and optimizes clinical performance across diverse populations.

ISIC 2020 and HAM10000 (Combined).notes: Statistical tests (McNemar's test and ANOVA) confirmed significant reductions in diagnostic inequality. The proposed model reduced false negatives for dark skin melanoma cases substantially. Fairness loss converged stably after ~30 epochs.

architecture_details.backbone: Vision Transformer (ViT) with patch embedding layer, multi-head self-attention blocks, and feed-forward layers to capture global contextual relationships and long-range dependencies.
architecture_details.fairness_module: Adversarial debiasing branch with an adversary network (A) that predicts skin tone from learned latent features (h) to enforce skin tone invariance.
architecture_details.loss_function: Combined task loss (L_task) and adversarial loss (L_adv) with a weighting factor beta: min_theta_f max_theta_A (L_task(f_theta_f) - beta * L_adv(A_theta_A(h))).
architecture_details.augmentation: StyleGAN-based generative augmentation for dark skin tones; DullRazor for hair artifact removal; color constancy correction; mean-variance normalization.

preprocessing_techniques: Hybrid skin tone clustering (automated color clustering combined with Fitzpatrick Skin Type scale approximation)
Color constancy correction for lighting normalization
Mean-variance normalization for consistent pixel scaling
Targeted augmentation (StyleGAN-based generative augmentation for dark skin tones)
Targeted re-sampling to address dataset imbalance

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to synthesize realistic lesion images on darker skin backgrounds to increase representation for underrepresented dark skin tones (FST V-VI) without duplicating existing samples.

Applied to address dataset imbalance in underrepresented dark skin classes. Re-sampling weight calculated as w_g = max(N_L, N_M, N_D) / N_g, where N_L, N_M, N_D are image counts for Light, Medium, and Dark skin groups respectively.
Used for hair artifact removal during preprocessing.

conclusions: The proposed fairness-enhanced Vision Transformer model significantly mitigates skin tone bias in melanoma detection, increasing dark skin sensitivity by 7.1% and boosting overall AUC to 0.931.

The multi-stage mitigation pipeline, combining hybrid skin tone clustering, targeted augmentation, re-sampling, fairness-aware loss, and adversarial debiasing, reduced major fairness disparities (accuracy gap, equalized odds difference, and FST wise variance) by approximately 50%.

Inclusion of fairness constraints not only mitigates skin tone bias but also optimizes overall clinical performance, improving accuracy by 1.8% and sensitivity by 2.5% compared to the baseline ViT Base model.
Statistical tests (McNemar's test and ANOVA) confirmed significant reductions in diagnostic inequality, validating the effectiveness of the proposed method.
The proposed model substantially reduces false negatives for dark skin melanoma cases, enhancing clinical safety and diagnostic equity for diverse populations.

Limitations acknowledged include the limited representation of dark skin in existing datasets and occasional artifacts in generative augmentation, indicating a need for more diverse clinical datasets and refined skin tone estimation for real-world generalizability.
important_remarks: The study relies on publicly available datasets (ISIC 2020 and HAM10000) which inherently suffer from underrepresentation of darker skin tones (Fitzpatrick IV–VI), limiting the generalizability of the mitigation strategies to truly diverse real-world populations.

Skin tone classification was performed using a hybrid method combining automated color clustering with Fitzpatrick Skin Type approximation; this approach is sensitive to variations in lighting and camera quality, which may introduce estimation errors in clinical deployment.

The mitigation pipeline employs StyleGAN-based generative augmentation to synthesize images for underrepresented dark skin tones; the authors acknowledge that synthetic data does not substitute for real biopsy-confirmed diverse datasets and note the presence of occasional GAN artifacts.

The paper acknowledges that segmentation models also exhibit strong bias against darker skin tones, suggesting that classification biases may be compounded by upstream segmentation failures, a factor not fully addressed in this classification-only study.

While statistical tests (McNemar's and ANOVA) confirmed significant reductions in diagnostic inequality, the study lacks external validation on independent, diverse clinical datasets, raising concerns about real-world robustness.

The proposed fairness-enhanced model improved dark skin sensitivity by 7.1% and reduced fairness disparities (accuracy gap, equalized odds difference) by approximately 50%, but residual performance differences between light and dark skin groups persisted.

The study highlights that many existing dermatology AI systems perform poorly on skin of color, with prior work showing ROC AUC drops of 27–36% on diverse datasets like DDI, underscoring the critical need for the addressed bias mitigation.

Limitations include variable label quality across datasets and the absence of richer clinical metadata (e.g., lesion location, disease subtypes), which limits transparency and stratified evaluation capabilities.
